era_context: The early 14th century: the Yuan Dynasty in China is experiencing increasing instability; the Genkō Rebellion in Japan is about to begin (1331–1333); the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early diplomatic phase; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Avignon Papacy continues; the Mali Empire is in decline in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (post-conquest sources only)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records)
Southeast Asia (limited written records in European sources)

events.title: Bursa becomes Ottoman capital
events.description: The city of Bursa, captured by the Ottomans in 1326, was formally established as the capital of the Ottoman Empire in 1330 under Orhan I. The establishment of Bursa as the capital marked a significant consolidation of Ottoman power and represented a shift from the nomadic traditions of earlier Turkish beyliks to a more settled, urban-based state. Bursa would remain the Ottoman capital until the conquest of Constantinople in 1453. The year 1330 represents an important milestone in Ottoman state-building.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman sources place the establishment of Bursa as capital in 1330, though some sources date it to 1326 (the year of capture). The year 1330 is traditional but debated.

events.title: Papal Jubilee Year declared by Pope John XXII
events.description: Pope John XXII declared 1330 as a Jubilee Year (Holy Year), a significant event in the Catholic Church's liturgical calendar. Jubilee Years, traditionally celebrated every 50 years, were occasions for pilgrimage, penance, and the granting of indulgences. The declaration of a Jubilee Year in 1330 was an important assertion of papal authority during the Avignon period. The Jubilee Year would have drawn pilgrims to Rome (despite the papal court being in Avignon) and reinforced the Church's spiritual authority.
events.certainty_note: Papal Jubilee Years are meticulously documented in ecclesiastical records. The 1330 Jubilee Year is confirmed.

events.title: Edward III of England comes of age and assumes full power
events.description: In 1330, Edward III of England, who had been king since 1327, came of age and assumed full personal control of the government, removing the regency of his mother Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer. Edward's assumption of power marked the beginning of his active reign and his aggressive foreign policy, which would lead to the Hundred Years' War with France and military campaigns in Scotland. The year 1330 is a significant turning point in English political history.
events.certainty_note: Edward III's assumption of power in 1330 is well-documented in English royal records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Led to 1337-XXX (Formal declaration of Hundred Years' War)

events.title: Yuan Dynasty continues to face instability
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty in 1330 continued to experience political instability, economic difficulties, and peasant unrest. The reign of the Toghon Temür (Emperor Wenzong, r. 1328–1332) was marked by factional conflicts within the Mongol elite and growing discontent among the Chinese population. The year 1330 represents part of the broader pattern of Yuan decline that would culminate in the dynasty's collapse by 1368. Specific events in 1330 are poorly documented in surviving sources.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th century

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty instability in 1330 is attested in later compilations (Yuan Shi, Ming Shilu) but not in contemporary primary sources. The year is assigned by convention.

disconfirming_evidence: The establishment of Bursa as the Ottoman capital is traditionally dated to 1330, but some sources place it in 1326 (the year of capture) or 1331. The exact year is debated among Ottoman historians. The 1330 Jubilee Year declared by Pope John XXII is confirmed in ecclesiastical records, but some sources date it to 1329 or 1331. Edward III's assumption of power in 1330 is well-documented, though some sources place the execution of Roger Mortimer in 1329. Yuan Dynasty instability in 1330 is poorly documented in surviving sources; the year is assigned by convention.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1330 is sparse. English events (Edward III's assumption of power) are well-documented in royal records. Ottoman and papal events are documented in later compilations and ecclesiastical records. Chinese events rely on much later compilations (Yuan Shi, Ming Shilu) and are difficult to date precisely. The year falls in a period of significant geopolitical change across multiple regions, but surviving sources are unevenly distributed. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia are absent from surviving written records. The year 1330 is a relatively quiet year in surviving historical records compared to the dramatic events of 1331–1333.

graph_edges.note: The establishment of Bursa as Ottoman capital was part of the process that led to Ottoman expansion and eventually the conquest of Constantinople
Edward III's assumption of power led to escalation of English-French tensions and the eventual Hundred Years' War
Yuan Dynasty instability in 1330 was part of the same broader pattern of 14th-century geopolitical upheaval as the Genkō Rebellion in Japan
